senses.definition: said as a response to another person's confident statement that one believes will be proven wrong in the end
to say something that is very soon proven wrong or inaccurate
to speak or offer one's opinions, particularly those affected by one's personal view or experience, in the way one sees fit
to assume without question that something is true

examples.x: I took it for granted that my car would start every morning until it finally broke down.
I didn't realize that Melanie hadn't been to college - I suppose I just took it for granted.
I suppose I just took it for granted that they were married.
You just take it for granted that people over a certain age can drive.

senses.definition: a series of connected thaughts and ideas, often formed to reach a proper conclusion

examples.x: Controlling your train of thoughts not only makes you more productive and effective in your work, it has other, more important benefits as well.
You'll know once you take control of your train of thought.
I lost my train of thought while giving the presentation and couldn't remember the next point.
Let me explain my train of thought so you can understand how I reached that decision.
His train of thought was so scattered during the conversation that it was hard to follow what he was saying.

senses.definition: something that is worth thinking about or considering deeply

examples.x: The thought-provoking article provided plenty of food for thought, prompting readers to reconsider their perspectives.
The speaker's insightful remarks during the conference gave the audience much food for thought.
The documentary presented a different perspective on the issue, offering viewers valuable food for thought.
The teacher posed a challenging question to the students, providing them with some food for thought before their discussion.
The book is filled with profound insights and philosophical ideas, making it a rich source of food for thought.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɑːnðə bɹˈeɪn

senses.definition: to be unable to stop talking or thinking about someone or something
used to suggest that if something accurately describes or applies to a person, they should accept it as true
something one is extremely obsessed with and keeps talking about
to continue to believe in something strongly

examples.x: Despite facing criticism, she held fast to her beliefs and refused to compromise her principles.
The team stood firm against their opponents' relentless attacks and managed to secure a hard-earned victory.
In the midst of uncertainty, he held fast to his dreams and worked tirelessly to achieve his goals.
The community stood firm in their demand for justice, organizing protests and refusing to back down.
Despite the difficult circumstances, the leader held firm and reassured the team with unwavering confidence.

senses.definition: (of praise, success, etc.) to make one feel too proud of oneself and degrade others

word_list.pronunciation: kˈʌm spɹˈɪŋ tʊ ˌɪntʊ wˈʌnz mˈaɪnd

senses.definition: (of an idea or thought) to suddenly be remembered or thought of

examples.x: When I heard the news, memories of our childhood summers immediately sprang to mind.
As I was walking through the park, the idea for a new painting came to mind.
When asked about my favorite book, "To Kill a Mockingbird" always comes to mind.
When I saw the old photograph, the image of my grandfather came into mind vividly.

senses.definition: something that a person imagines is real but in fact it is not

examples.x: The ghostly figure he claimed to have seen was merely a figment of his imagination; there was no evidence of any supernatural presence.
The elaborate conspiracy theory turned out to be a figment of his imagination, lacking any factual basis.
The idea that he would fail miserably and be ridiculed by others was just a figment of his imagination; he had the skills and abilities to succeed.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐtˈætʃ ɐ lˈeɪbəl tʊ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to describe or think of someone or something in specific way

word_list.pronunciation: ɪn ɐ wˈɜːld ʌv wˈʌnz ˈoʊn

senses.definition: mainly focusing on one's thoughts and not paying much attention to what is happening around one

examples.x: Lost in her music, she sat in a corner, completely in a world of her own.
The young girl, with her nose buried in a book, was in a world of her own, oblivious to the noise around her.
The artist immersed in painting was in a world of his own, capturing his imagination on the canvas.
Engrossed in his thoughts, he walked through the park, seemingly in a world of his own.
The daydreamer gazed out the window, appearing to be in a world of her own, disconnected from reality.

senses.definition: to carefully think about what someone else has said

examples.x: As a diplomat, he always weighs his words before making any public statements.
When giving advice, it's crucial to weigh your words to ensure they are helpful and empathetic.
She carefully weighed her words during the interview, aware of the potential consequences of her responses.
After a heated argument, they took a moment to calm down and weigh their words before continuing the discussion.
The politician, aware of the sensitive topic, chose to weigh his words in order to maintain public trust.

senses.definition: with one's thoughts or concerns centered on a particular subject or issue

examples.x: With the upcoming exam, studying is constantly on my mind.
The recent job interview is still on her mind; she's eagerly awaiting the outcome.
The health scare has been weighing heavily on his mind, causing sleepless nights.
Planning the wedding has been on their minds for months; it's a major focus of their conversations.

senses.definition: used to indicate that after a key event, the following developments are well-known or self-explanatory

examples.x: He launched his first product in his garage, and the rest is history—the company became a global powerhouse.
She auditioned for the lead role in the musical, and the rest is history—she became a Broadway star.
The band released their debut album, and the rest is history—they went on to sell millions of records.
They met by chance at a party, and the rest is history—they've been inseparable ever since.
They took a risk and invested in the startup, and the rest is history—the company became a unicorn.

senses.definition: to say the exact thing someone else was about to say or was thinking of

word_list.word: to [judge] a book by its cover
word_list.pronunciation: dʒˈʌdʒ ɐ bˈʊk baɪ ɪts kˈʌvɚ

senses.definition: to form an opinion or make a judgment about something or someone based solely on its outward appearance or initial impression

examples.x: Don't judge a book by its cover; she may seem reserved, but she has a wealth of knowledge and experiences to share.
The dilapidated storefront may not look appealing, but don't judge a book by its cover—the food inside is absolutely delicious.
He appeared confident and put-together, but when I got to know him better, I realized I had judged a book by its cover; he was actually quite insecure.
The plain packaging may not catch your eye, but don't judge a book by its cover; the product inside is of exceptional quality.
Despite his unconventional appearance, don't judge a book by its cover; he is an incredibly talented musician.

senses.definition: used of a person whose mind is busy with the thought of a particular person or thing and cannot think of anyone or anything else

examples.x: He has a one-track mind when it comes to fitness; he's constantly talking about workouts and nutrition.
She has a one-track mind for success; her entire focus is on climbing the corporate ladder.
Whenever he starts a new hobby, he gets a one-track mind and immerses himself completely in it.
His one-track mind for money often leads him to make questionable decisions in pursuit of wealth.
She's so infatuated with her new romantic interest that she has a one-track mind and can't seem to think about anything else.

word_list.word: there is more to {sb/sth} than (what|) meets the eye
word_list.pronunciation: ðɛɹ ɪz mˈoːɹ tʊ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ðɐn wˌʌt ɔːɹ mˈiːts ðɪ ˈaɪ

senses.definition: used to suggest that there are hidden or undiscovered aspects to a person or thing that may be surprising or unexpected
